summary: Julia Evans shares some of her favorite technical blogs and explains what makes each one special. She highlights Rachel by the Bay for sysadmin war stories, Dan Luu for hardware writing, Aphyr for distributed systems analysis, and Camille Fournier for engineering management reflections. The common thread she identifies is that all these bloggers write about what they're currently working on or learning, taking readers along for the ride. She closes with a wish for more people to write blogs sharing their ongoing work and thinking.
key_points: The best technical blogs share the author's current work and learning process rather than polished retrospectives
Great technical bloggers cover topics at a depth that creates learning opportunities readers wouldn't find elsewhere
Following a challenging blog over time can gradually build understanding of difficult concepts like distributed systems
Linking to specific posts rather than homepages is a more useful way to recommend blogs
money_quotes: What I love about all of these blogs (and what I strive for in my own writing) is that they all (at least sometimes) write about what they're currently working on or learning about.
They're all by people who are very knowledgeable, and take you along for the ride as they're learning and experimenting.
My constant wish with blogging is for more people to write blogs where they tell me what they're working on and what they're thinking about.

I honestly struggled with reading it – the concepts he talks about are difficult to understand if you don't have a lot of experience with distributed systems. But having followed him for a couple of years now, I've learned a ton.
This post about whether or not you should use error-correcting memory is a topic I would have no idea where to start learning about if it were not for his blog.
key_insight: The most valuable technical blogs are written by knowledgeable people who share what they're actively working on and learning, inviting readers along for the journey.
